title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a river clean-up project. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Helena, The Waste-Sorting Lead
Helena volunteered at the river clean-up after seeing photographs of plastic bags trapped in the reeds near her flat. She was assigned to the sorting area, where bags from different teams were opened and checked. [47]Her most worrying discovery was a leaking container with no label, which she separated from the general rubbish until the council safety officer arrived.[/47] That incident made everyone take gloves and reporting rules and warning tape more seriously. [48]Her laboratory experience helped her set up separate zones for glass, metal, sharp objects and unknown liquids without creating unnecessary panic.[/48] Helena also recorded the strangest items on a whiteboard, partly to keep volunteers interested during the repetitive work. By the end of the morning, she was less shocked by the quantity of litter than by how quickly people learned careful habits. She says cleaning is satisfying, but sorting correctly is what turns effort into useful evidence.
texts.B:
### Jack, The Boat Marshal
Jack regularly helped coordinate the small boats used to collect rubbish from the opposite bank. [49]He became involved when his fishing club was asked to provide volunteers who understood the river’s currents.[/49] He thought the job would simply involve pointing people towards floating bottles and polystyrene trays, but the river decided otherwise within minutes. [50]He was surprised by how much the tide changed the safe launch times and the distance boats could travel before turning back.[/50] Jack carried a whistle and kept notes on which crews had radios and spare oars. He annoyed some enthusiastic volunteers by refusing to let them continue after the turning point, but nobody capsized or needed towing. He also learned that rubbish hidden under branches was often easier to reach from land than from the water. Jack says patience matters on a river, because forcing the schedule usually means someone else has to rescue your mistake later.
texts.C:
### Priya, The Education Table Volunteer
Priya ran the education table beside the busy riverside path, showing families what had been collected and explaining how litter reached the sea. She had planned a dramatic display of objects found in the mud. [51]After several children tried to pick up rusty hooks and broken plastic shards, she replaced the open display with sealed jars and laminated photographs.[/51] The whole table became safer and more attractive. Priya invited children to guess how long different items might remain in river water, then revealed the answers with coloured cards. [52]A quiet boy asked whether fish could mistake a birthday balloon for food, and his father later said the question changed their next family party.[/52] Priya found that environmental messages worked best when they began with curiosity rather than guilt or frightening statistics. She also gave teachers a simple follow-up activity, so the clean-up would continue in classrooms after the volunteers left.
texts.D:
### Marcus, The Wildlife Recorder
Marcus joined the clean-up because he had watched kingfishers on the same river as a child and wanted them to return. [53]That childhood memory of seeing kingfishers made the project feel personal rather than abstract.[/53] His role was to record wildlife signs before and after volunteers moved through each section. [54]He marked quiet zones around nesting areas so teams would not drag bags or shout near the reeds.[/54] Some volunteers found the restrictions frustrating, but Marcus explained that cleaning should not damage what it meant to protect. [55]He created a simple map showing where litter could be removed immediately and where it had to be reported for specialist access.[/55] The map prevented arguments when people spotted rubbish beyond safe reach. [56]Marcus is training two students to continue the surveys, because wildlife records only become valuable when collected consistently over several seasons.[/56] He hopes future reports will show more than cleaner banks.

questions.question: found a potentially dangerous unidentified item and kept it apart from ordinary rubbish?
questions.explanation: Helena separated a leaking unlabelled container until the safety officer arrived.

questions.question: used scientific work experience to organise waste safely into different areas?
questions.explanation: Helena’s laboratory experience helped her create zones for different waste types.

questions.question: joined because a club they belonged to was asked for specialist volunteers?
questions.explanation: Jack became involved when his fishing club was asked for volunteers.

questions.question: was surprised by how water movement affected when the work could be done safely?
questions.explanation: Jack found the tide changed safe launch times and travel distance.

questions.question: changed a public display after children tried to handle unsafe objects?
questions.explanation: Priya replaced an open display after children touched rusty hooks and sharp plastic.

questions.question: recalls a child’s question influencing a family’s future behaviour?
questions.explanation: A boy’s question about balloons changed his family’s next party.

questions.question: was motivated by a childhood memory of wildlife at the site?
questions.explanation: Marcus remembered watching kingfishers on the river as a child.

questions.question: created areas where volunteers had to work quietly to avoid disturbing animals?
questions.explanation: Marcus marked quiet zones around nesting areas.

questions.question: made a map distinguishing places volunteers could act from places needing specialist help?
questions.explanation: Marcus mapped where litter could be removed immediately or reported for specialist access.

questions.question: is teaching others to continue collecting information over several seasons?
questions.explanation: Marcus is training students to continue wildlife surveys consistently.
